coredata.dc:title: Extraction and modification of cigarette smoke aerosol derived nanoparticle for supercapacitor

coredata.dc:creator: Maity, Chandan Kumar
Acharya, Sourav
De, Shrabani
Tiwari, Santosh Kumar
Kumari, Khusboo
Joy, Anupama
Sahoo, Satyanarayan
Sahoo, Sumanta
Nayak, Ganesh Chandra
coredata.dc:description: Developing sustainable solutions for utilizing toxic pollutants in different applications is a significant challenge. This article explores how cigarette smoke—an aerosol containing numerous hazardous organic compounds and metals—was captured and transformed into metal and carbon-based nanoparticles (carbon dots) through a straightforward hydrothermal process. Cigarette smoke extract was prepared in water and then hydrothermally treated to obtain solid particulates. These metal and carbon-based nanoparticles were subsequently combined with carbon black and carbon nanotubes to enhance their stability and electrochemical activity. An asymmetric supercapacitor was developed by utilizing these modified nanoparticles to demonstrate the potential of cigarette smoke aerosol-derived nanomaterials for energy storage. Photoluminescence and TEM analysis confirmed the formation of carbon dots, while FESEM and elemental mapping validated the presence of metal-based nanoparticles. The asymmetric supercapacitor device was fabricated with carbon black modified aerosol-derived carbon and metal-based nanoparticles achieved a specific capacitance of 161 F/g along with energy and power density of 72 W h/kg and 1798 W/kg, at 1 A/g current density, respectively. Overall, this study illustrates that capturing and converting hazardous aerosols can simultaneously tackle two critical issues: environmental pollution and the demand for energy storage, using a simple and scalable approach applicable to various toxic aerosol systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Toxic pollutants
Cigarette smoke aerosol
Photoluminescence
Carbon dots
Asymmetric supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterizations of different types of cigarette solutions (WBH, WBHD, WAH, and WAHD)
Morphological analysis of cigarette particles and their composites
Application of toxic waste cigarette smoke for supercapacitor device
Electrochemical evaluations of asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Apparatus used for making aqueous extracts of cigarette smoke aerosol; and digital photographs of cigarette aerosol extract in water.

(a) UV–Vis spectra of before hydrothermal solutions, (b) UV–Vis spectra of after hydrothermal solutions, (c) PL spectra of before hydrothermal solutions, and (d) PL spectra of after hydrothermal solutions of different cigarette extracts.

Digital images of after hydrothermal solution of individual cigarette extracts (one to five-cigarette extract) in normal light (a), and under UV-light (b); TEM images of (c, d) CDs produced from cigarette extract (Inset image of panel (d) shows the lattice fringes).
FESEM images of (a, b) cigarette particle, and (c, d) cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite in lower and higher magnification.
(a) HAADF image of cigarette particle, and (b–i) Elemental mapping of C, S, O, Cl, Al, Fe, Se, and Si in cigarette particle, respectively.

Electrochemical analysis of carbon black (CB), cigarette particle (CP), cigarette particle-CB ex-situ (CP CB Ex), cigarette particle-CNT in-situ (CP CNT In), and cigarette particle-CB in-situ (CP CB In) composites in three-electrode system using 1 M TEABF4/ACN: (a) CV plots at 1 mV/s, (b) CV plots of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite at different scan rates (1–10 mV/s), (c) GCD plots at 1 A/g, (d) GCD plots of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite at various current densities (1 to 4 A/g), (e) Coulombic efficiency and cyclic stability plots of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite, and (f) Fitted Nyquist plot of all active materials.

Electrochemical measurements of the ASC: (a) CV graphs at different potential windows, (b) CV graphs at multiple scan rates within 0–1.8 V, (c) GCD graphs at different potential windows, (d) GCD plots at multiple current densities within 0–1.8 V, (e) Nyquist plot of the device (inset shows equivalent circuit), and (f) Ragone plot of the device with a comparison of other reported works.

Practical application of cigarette particle-CB in-situ//CB device using carbon fiber current collector: (a, b) Running of a 2 V fan with respect to different times and (c–f) Pictorial images of the device to illuminate 43 red-LEDs (‘IIT ISM’) with respect to time. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Process followed for the extraction, modification, and application of cigarette smoke aerosol for supercapacitor application.

head.ce:title: Extraction and modification of cigarette smoke aerosol derived nanoparticle for supercapacitor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing sustainable solutions for utilizing toxic pollutants in different applications is a significant challenge. This article explores how cigarette smoke—an aerosol containing numerous hazardous organic compounds and metals—was captured and transformed into metal and carbon-based nanoparticles (carbon dots) through a straightforward hydrothermal process. Cigarette smoke extract was prepared in water and then hydrothermally treated to obtain solid particulates. These metal and carbon-based nanoparticles were subsequently combined with carbon black and carbon nanotubes to enhance their stability and electrochemical activity. An asymmetric supercapacitor was developed by utilizing these modified nanoparticles to demonstrate the potential of cigarette smoke aerosol-derived nanomaterials for energy storage. Photoluminescence and TEM analysis confirmed the formation of carbon dots, while FESEM and elemental mapping validated the presence of metal-based nanoparticles. The asymmetric supercapacitor device was fabricated with carbon black modified aerosol-derived carbon and metal-based nanoparticles achieved a specific capacitance of 161 F/g along with energy and power density of 72 W h/kg and 1798 W/kg, at 1 A/g current density, respectively. Overall, this study illustrates that capturing and converting hazardous aerosols can simultaneously tackle two critical issues: environmental pollution and the demand for energy storage, using a simple and scalable approach applicable to various toxic aerosol systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of several organic molecules in cigarette smoke aerosol can be explored for the synthesis of CDs by trapping aerosols within a solvent and subsequent heat treatment [20]. During the heat treatment, numerous organic compounds degrade into smaller fragments of carbon dots and graphitic carbon dots. Moreover, the presence of different non-metal components like sulfur and nitrogen can also produce doped CDs, and the presence of aluminum, lead, copper, chromium, cadmium, mercury, nickel, and zinc in cigarette smoke aerosol can produce their corresponding metal oxides simultaneously during the heat treatment. Since metal oxides like ZnO, CuO, NiO, PbO2, CrO3, etc. have been widely used for supercapacitors due to their superior electrochemical performances, the formation of these metal oxides during heat treatment of smoke aerosol can enhance the pseudocapacitance, through redox reactions [21–24].

In this article, a sustainable approach has been developed to convert toxic smoke aerosol to metal and carbon-based nanoparticles and their subsequent modifications for the preparation of efficient electrode materials for supercapacitor devices. Cigarette smoke has been used as the source of toxic smoke aerosol to demonstrate the efficiency of this methodology. Cigarette smoke aerosol was passed through the water to produce water extract of cigarette smoke aerosol. This extract was then hydrothermally treated in the presence and absence of carbon black (CB) and carbon nanotube (CNT), respectively, to produce a metal and carbon-based electrode for a supercapacitor. Different organic components with π-electron conjugation of cigarette smoke can interact with the CB and CNT through π-π interactions, leading to the stabilization of the carbon-based electrode. This approach can be applied to mitigate two of the major problems i.e. the reduction of toxicity and polluting effects of smoke aerosols and the fabrication of cheap energy storage devices.

For this experiment, commercially available cigarettes (Silkcut Virginia, ITC, India) were purchased from the local market. Carbon black (CB) and CNT were procured from Alfa Aesar, India. and Nanocyl, Belgium, respectively.

The aqueous extracts of one cigarette, without the filter portion was prepared by passing the smoke from the burning cigarette through water using a glass apparatus, as shown in Fig. 1. A burning cigarette was attached to one end of the apparatus, and its smoke was passed through the main chamber filled with 65 ml of water and a filter system connected to a vacuum pump at the other end. The smoke passing through the water got adsorbed resulting in the formation of a brown solution. Fig. 1 also displays the digital image of cigarette smoke extract in water. On the other hand, the Supporting movie 1 demonstrates the methodology of cigarette smoke extraction process. A similar procedure was followed for obtaining extracts from two, three, four, and five cigarettes, respectively.

The aqueous extracts of one cigarette, without the filter portion was prepared by passing the smoke from the burning cigarette through water using a glass apparatus, as shown in Fig. 1. A burning cigarette was attached to one end of the apparatus, and its smoke was passed through the main chamber filled with 65 ml of water and a filter system connected to a vacuum pump at the other end. The smoke passing through the water got adsorbed resulting in the formation of a brown solution. Fig. 1 also displays the digital image of cigarette smoke extract in water. On the other hand, the Supporting movie 1 demonstrates the methodology of cigarette smoke extraction process. A similar procedure was followed for obtaining extracts from two, three, four, and five cigarettes, respectively.

At first, aqueous cigarette smoke extract (30 ml) was transferred into a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and was heated for 8 h at 180 °C. The remaining solution (35 ml) was labeled as the “before hydrothermal solution” (WBH). After hydrothermal treatment, the particles obtained were collected through centrifugation and the solution denoted as "after hydrothermal solution" (WAH). The particles thus obtained were denoted as Cigarette Particle (CP) for the rest of the manuscript. The same procedure was followed for two, three, four, and five cigarette extracts. Finally, two separate solutions were obtained from each set of cigarette extracts, i.e., “before the hydrothermal solution” (WBH1, WBH2, WBH3, WBH4, WBH5) and “after the hydrothermal solution” (WAH1, WAH2, WAH3, WAH4, WAH5). After characterization, WBHs of all cigarette extracts were distilled and the distillates were collected (WBHD). The same procedure was followed for WAHs of all cigarette extracts and the distillates were collected (WAHD). Finally, four different types of solutions (WBH, WAH, WBHD, and WAHD) were prepared from each set of cigarette extract. Digital images of different types of cigarette solutions (WBH and WAH) are displayed in Fig. S1(a, b).

For the synthesis of the cigarette particle composite, the aqueous extract of five cigarettes was only used. To prepare the cigarette particle (CP)‑carbon black (CB) composite, both in-situ and ex-situ processes were followed. In the in-situ process, 20 ml of the aqueous cigarette extract obtained from five cigarettes was taken and 20 mg of CB was dispersed into this solution through 30 min of ultrasonication. The mixture was then transferred to a 50-ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and subsequently heated for 8 h at 180 °C. After the completion of the hydrothermal process, black particles obtained were collected through centrifugation. The yield of the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite was ~36 mg. During the ex-situ process, initially, 20 ml of the aqueous cigarette extract (of five cigarettes) was heated at 180 °C for 8 h, and cigarette particles, thus obtained were collected. Then, 20 mg CB was mixed with these cigarette particles through external mixing. For cigarette particle-CNT in-situ composite, the above-mentioned procedure for cigarette particle-CB was followed, by taking CNT in place of CB.

Details of material characterization techniques and electrochemical characterization are discussed in S1 (supporting information). The detailed process followed for the application of Cigarette smoke extract for supercapacitor application including its different modifications is shown schematically in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of different types of cigarette solutions (WBH, WBHD, WAH, and WAHD)
ce:section.ce:para: Smoke aerosol is a complex combination of several organic and inorganic components where the latter part is minor in quantity but electrochemically more active due to various redox activities. It is evident that, whether the metal part can be converted to corresponding metal oxides in a facile manner, breaking down organic components into corresponding carbon nanoparticles is quite challenging. Hence, there are two major questions to be answered for the conversion of smoke aerosol to carbon-based nanoparticles (carbon dots), (1) complete breakdown of organic components and (2) formation of carbon dots. To confirm the formation of carbon dots and to analyze the efficiency of hydrothermal treatment (for the production of carbon dots from smoke aerosol), the optical characteristics of the aqueous extracts of cigarette smoke were evaluated by UV–visible Spectrophotometric analysis. The WBH of one to five cigarettes, depicted a strong absorption peak at 261 nm presented in Fig. 2(a). The intensity of the peaks progressively diminished from five to one cigarette smoke solution respectively as the concentration of the dissolved organic components decreased with the reducing number of cigarettes. The peak at 261 nm is because of the electronic transition from π-π*, indicating the presence of organic components having π-electrons [25,26]. The absorption peaks for the WAH of these cigarette extracts (Fig. 2(b)) were found at 260 nm and the nature of the spectrum was similar to that of WBH. When both the before and after hydrothermal solutions of one to five cigarette extracts were distilled and their absorption spectra were recorded, the peaks in the same region (260 nm) were observed as shown in Fig. S2(a, b). Therefore, it is concluded that all components present in WBH solutions are not decomposed during the hydrothermal or distillation process.

One of the most important properties of the cigarette extracts is their fluorescence property, which is determined by their PL study. The cigarette smoke contains various types of organic compounds which show fluorescence behavior in the solution phase. PL study of the WBH (Fig. 2(c)) and WAH (Fig. 2(d)) solutions of individual cigarette extracts (one to five-cigarette extract) were studied at 260 nm, which showed a sharp emission peak centered at 420 nm. The distillate WBH solutions were also excited at the same wavelength resulting in a sharp emission peak at 420 nm for all cigarette extracts as shown in Fig. S3(a). For “after hydrothermal” distillates, these solutions possess a sharp peak centered at 460 nm, (Fig. S3(b)) when these solutions were excited at 260 nm which is in the blue region [27,28]. The emission peaks for the “after hydrothermal” distillates were shifted towards a higher wavelength by about 40 nm. The intensity of the peak in PL spectra depends upon the concentration of the solution, and the concentration of the cigarette extract increases with increasing the number of cigarettes.

Under UV light of 365 nm wavelength, all forms of aqueous cigarette extracts exhibited vivid blue luminescence that can be visualized with the naked eye. Fig. 3(a) shows the “after-hydrothermal” solution of individual cigarette extracts (one to five cigarette extracts) in normal light. However, Under UV light, these solutions displayed intense blue luminescence, as represented in Fig. 3(b). This phenomenon demonstrates the production of CDs in the solution phase of cigarette smoke. The existence of CDs in cigarette solutions is responsible for the PL peak for the extracts. From the TEM images, the generation of CDs is also visible. Fig. 3(c, d) displays the TEM images of CDs produced by the hydrothermal process using cigarette extract. With an average diameter of 6 nm, monodispersed homogeneous CDs are formed. Additionally, it should be noted that CDs have a lattice fringe of 0.22 nm, which is equivalent to the (110) plane of graphitic carbon (inset image of Fig. 3(d)) [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological analysis of cigarette particles and their composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a–d) and Fig. S4(a, b) show the FESEM image of cigarette particles (produced from five-cigarette extract via hydrothermal treatment), cigarette particle-CB, and cigarette particle-CNT composites generated in situ. The surface morphology of cigarette particles obtained from hydrothermal treatment and via incorporation of CB and CNT with cigarette particles by the in-situ process is visualized from these FESEM images. Cube-like structures of cigarette particles have been observed from FESEM analysis at low and high magnification images, as illustrated in Fig. 4(a) and (b), correspondingly. The cube-like structure may be ascribed to the generation of metal oxide or metal-organic framework during hydrothermal treatment, as cigarette smoke contains metal oxides as well as metal-containing species. The presence of metals in cigarette particles is confirmed by EDX analysis (Fig. S5(a)). During the hydrothermal process, metals or metal oxides included in cigarette smoke interact with the solvent molecules leading to rapid growth of nanocrystals which results in the loss of surface energy and the formation of cubes by self-assembly mechanism [30]. These cigarette particles are deposited uniformly on CB when they form composites with CB via in-situ hydrothermal treatment. The uniform deposition is clearly visible from both higher and lower magnification FESEM images, as displayed in Fig. 4(c, d). In the case of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite, CB acts as a template; furthermore, the nucleation and growth of the cigarette particles occur on the surface of the CB during the hydrothermal treatment. Therefore, the size of the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite has become smaller than the cigarette particle, as evident from the FESEM analysis. On the other hand, cigarette particles are wrapped on the tubular CNT when cigarette extract is treated hydrothermally with CNT which is observed from its corresponding FESEM images in Fig. S4(a, b).

From the FESEM images of both cigarette particle-based in-situ composites, it can be concluded that the deposition and interaction of the cigarette particle-CB composite is better as compared to the cigarette particle-CNT composite. This is because the particle-particle interaction is more feasible than the particle-CNT interaction. Notably, the uniform deposition and better interaction can facilitate the penetration of the electrolyte ions as well as electron transport during the electrochemical process.

Elemental mapping of the cigarette particulate obtained from the hydrothermal treatment was performed through the TEM analysis. Fig. 5(a) depicts the HAADF image of cigarette particles. The mapping of this specific area displays the existence of elements like C, S, O, Cl, Al, Fe, Se, and Si. (Fig. 5(b–i)). The major components present in cigarette smoke are organic compounds which can be confirmed by the whole covering of C. The presence of S and Cl-containing components in cigarette smoke was also confirmed by the spatial distribution of S and Cl. The mapping of particular areas of the cigarette particles also confirms the presence of different metals like Fe, Al, and Si. XPS analysis was carried out to confirm the elements present in cigarette particles. Fig. S5(b) shows the XPS survey plot of cigarette particles and it confirms the occurrence of C, S, F, Cl, N, and O with atomic % of 70.9, 2.0, 2.8, 0.3, 1.6, and 22.5 %, respectively. Therefore, it can be concluded that cigarette particles mainly contain S, F, Cl, and N-based organic components. Fig. S5(c) shows the XRD plot of the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite which clearly shows the peaks of carbon at around 2θ = 26° and 44°.

The deposition of cigarette particles on the CB is also confirmed from the TEM and HRTEM images of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite (Fig. S6). The TEM images of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite clearly show that cigarette particles are deposited on the surface of CB (Fig. S6(a, b)). The high-resolution TEM images (Fig. S6(c, d)) show the smooth surface and amorphous feature of the original carbon particles, on which cigarette particles are deposited. Feasible interaction of CB with cigarette particles may increase more active sites, and improve interfacial charge transportation, and desired surface area during the electrochemical process.

High surface area is a vital parameter for facilitating surface charge storage in supercapacitor electrode materials. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite was also performed through the N2 adsorption-desorption method to measure the specific surface area and pore size distribution (Fig. S7). The calculated BET surface area of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite was found to be 74.11 m2/g. The mesoporous nature of the electrode material plays a vital role for the electrochemical performances. The pore size distribution plot of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite (inset of Fig. S7) indicates the mesoporous characteristics of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite, which is very effective for electrochemical charge storage via electrolyte ions diffusion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of toxic waste cigarette smoke for supercapacitor device
ce:section.ce:para: The particulate collected after hydrothermal treatment was used to fabricate the electrode material for the supercapacitors. For the enhancement of electrochemical efficiency as well as the conductivity of the system, in-situ composites of cigarette particles with CB and CNT were prepared on a larger scale. The ex-situ cigarette particle-CB composites were also prepared to compare the electrochemical activity with the in-situ composites. Initially, all electroactive materials were electrochemically characterized in a three-electrode arrangement for their use in supercapacitors. In addition, an asymmetric supercapacitor device prepared from the best electrode material was assessed for electrochemical efficacy. In both instances, 1 M solution of TEABF4/Acetonitrile (ACN) was used as the electrolyte.

Employing a traditional three-electrode configuration, CV analysis of all the electroactive materials was tested within 0.0 V to 1.6 V potential limit. CV analysis of only cigarette particles (CP) was carried out at multiple scan rates (1–10 mV/s), as displayed in Fig. S8(a). Throughout the potentiodynamic investigation, distinct redox peaks in both positive and negative sweeps were visualized at multiple scan rates. These redox peaks confirmed the predominate pseudocapacitive nature of the obtained cigarette particle (91 %), which is from the presence of metal oxides and other pseudocapacitive materials (Fig. S11). Using Trasatti's approach, this factor was evaluated and confirmed based on theoretical calculations (S2, supporting information). The area of the CV curves enlarged with increasing the scan rates, due to the quick diffusion kinetics over the electrode surface [31]. At a higher scan rate, the electrolyte does not get adequate time to interact with the surface of the electrode but at a lower scan rate, more time is required to record the voltammogram. Notably, low scan rates result in the formation of diffusion layers away from the electrode surface, which lowers the electron flow towards the electrode surface as compared to high scan rates. Therefore, the scan rate must be highly effective towards the current density. Fig. 6(a) portrays the CV profiles of all electroactive materials (carbon black (CB), only cigarette particles, cigarette particles composed with CB & CNT via in-situ process, and cigarette particles coated with CB through ex-situ process) at the scan rate of 1 mV/s. The area of the CV curve is directly correlated with the C
sc of any electrode material. The CV area is maximum for cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite at 1 mV/s as compared with others, and thus, it showed the best capacitive nature. All the CV profiles possess redox peaks implying pseudocapacitive behavior of the electrode materials due to the presence of different types of metal oxides in the cigarette particle. The CV analysis of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite at different scan rates is represented in Fig. 6(b). With increasing scan rate, the area of the CV curves and current density were found to be increased. For cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite, fast reversible faradic reactions and perfect capacitive characteristics are shown by the quasi-rectangular shape and improved current density of the CV curve at higher scan rates. The absence of a well-defined redox peak in cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite is mainly due to the incorporation of EDLC-type CB, which supports the mechanism for storing surface charges. Due to the incorporation of EDLC-type material, the contribution of EDLC has been increased up to 27 % for cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite as compared to only cigarette particles (Fig. S11). On the other hand, the CV plots of cigarette particle-CB ex-situ and cigarette particle-CNT in-situ composites at different scan rates (1–10 mV/s) are portrayed in Fig. S8(b, c), correspondingly. From the theoretical calculations (S2, supporting information), it can be observed that the EDLC and pseudocapacitance contribution of cigarette particle-CB ex-situ composite is almost equal. The area of the CV curve and current density of the composite electrode materials have been increased compared to the cigarette particle. High conducting nature and EDLC contribution of CB and CNT are the key factors in increasing the current response and CV area at that scan rate. In addition, the incorporation of CB and CNT generates more active sites for the diffusion of the electrolyte ions thus enhancing electrochemical activity. Better interaction of CB and cigarette particles at the molecular level and uniform deposition is responsible for the highest capacitive performances of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite.

GCD analysis was performed in the same electrode-electrolyte system utilizing a potential window identical to that of CV analysis to determine the C
sc and look into the real-time consumption of all electroactive components. The charging-discharge time is one of the most predominating factors for supercapacitor electrodes with the C
sc of an electrode material calculated from discharge time. In a three-electrode system, the C
sc of all the electroactive materials was calculated from the discharge time of GCD profiles using the eq. S1(supporting information) [32–34]. The smart combination of EDLC and pseudocapacitance can give a longer discharge time and this phenomenon might be demonstrated in a typical hybrid electrochemical supercapacitor. The charging-discharging of all electroactive materials was carried out at 1 A/g and the corresponding GCD plot is displayed in Fig. 6(c). At this particular current density, cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite depicted the maximum discharge time duration as compared to others. The electrochemical performance of all the electroactive materials was calculated by calculating the C
sc from the discharge time obtained from GCD analysis. The C
sc of CB, cigarette particle, cigarette particle-CB ex-situ, cigarette particle-CNT in-situ, and cigarette particle-CB in-situ composites were found to be 30, 115.6, 98.8, 118.8, and 248.8 F/g respectively, at 1 A/g. The GCD curves' characteristics essentially showed the electrodes' pseudocapacitive nature, which was amply supported by the findings of the CV analysis and theoretical calculations. GCD study of the electrode materials shows that all electrode materials, except bare cigarette particles, have Coulombic efficiencies of more than 100 %. Cigarette particle-CB in-situ composites showed the highest discharge time corresponding to the highest C
sc , which correlates with the results of CV analysis. Due to the use of highly conductive CB and CNT and improved molecular interaction during hydrothermal treatment, the C
sc for in-situ composites has been greatly boosted compared to only cigarette particles. However, better electrochemical activity and higher C
sc of CB in-situ composite than CNT composite can be interpreted as a result of the accumulation of surface charge and particle size. From ZETA potential measurement, it was observed that cigarette particles composited with CB (in-situ) (Fig. S12(a)) accumulate more surface charge as compared to cigarette particle-CNT in-situ composite (Fig. S12(b)). The mean particle size of the in-situ CB composite was found to be lesser than the in-situ CNT composite as confirmed by the DLS study (Fig. S13(a, b)). Small particle size possesses a high surface area so the material can accumulate more charge on the surface during the electrochemical process. Each of these processes effectively contributes to the electrochemical performance of the in-situ cigarette particle-CB composite. The GCD analysis of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite at different current densities was also tested and the respective GCD plot is shown in Fig. 6d. In a three-electrode system, the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite showed the C
s of 248.8, 177.5, 88, and 75 F/g at 1, 2, 3, and 4 A/g, respectively. The In-situ composite made of cigarette particles and CB maintained moderate capacitance even at higher current densities.

The electrodes in supercapacitors should have long-term cyclic stability. Consequently, GCD analysis (depicted in Fig. 6(e)) of the best electrode material (cigarette particle-CB in-situ) was performed up to 10,000 cycles within the range similar to that of CV and GCD analyses. Even after 10,000 cycles, the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite maintained ~71 % of its capacitance. Following the cyclic stability test, the morphology of the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite was further examined, as shown in Fig. S14(a, b). From the FESEM images, it was evidenced that the morphological structure of the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite has not changed in any noticeable way after cyclic stability. The electrolyte ions' progressive entry into the pores caused the electrode to become activated, which in turn increased the capacitance retention initially. However, the general decrease in retention may be interpreted by the structural distortion of the pseudocapacitive materials and the partial separation of the electrode material and current collector because of the continuous disinsertion and insertion of the ions of the electrolyte. However, the Coulombic efficiencies (Fig. 6(e)) of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite were more than 100 %, and this is mainly due to the additional electrode reactions, such as the decomposition of the electrolyte, during the discharging process.

Investigating the electrochemical behavior of the electroactive materials (charge transport characteristics and capacitance) was the primary goal of the electrochemical impedance and the equivalent Nyquist plot is depicted in Fig. 6(f). The Nyquist plot was used to assign the imaginary component (−Z') of impedance versus its real component (Z"). The analogous circuit provided a decent fit to the Nyquist plot, as portrayed in Fig. S15. The combined effects of double-layer capacitance and charge transfer, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) is responsible for the semicircles found in the high-frequency area of the spectrum, and the mass transfer phenomena that occur during an electrochemical progression is represented by the straight line in the low-frequency zone [35,36]. Low-frequency region is associated with phenomena such as adsorption, tiny charge transfer, and surface roughness [26,37]. The diameter of the arc can be used to calculate the Rct. A larger semicircle was obtained for the cigarette particle compared to others, which indicated the poorly conducting nature of this material and poor conductivity increases electrode and electrolyte interfacial charge transfer resistance significantly. The corresponding Rct values of the cigarette particle, cigarette particle-CB ex-situ, cigarette particle-CNT in-situ, and cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite were found to be 7.44, 6.78, 6.59, 6.51 Ω, respectively. Rct values decreased for the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite and the cigarette particle-CNT in-situ composite. The high conductivity of the CB and CNT is positively responsible for decreasing the charge transfer resistance as the incorporation of these carbonaceous materials makes the electron transport feasible. However, the lowest Rct value for the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite is due to the high surface charge accumulation, which is well supported by ZETA potential measurement. The composites' equivalent series resistance (RESR) is represented by the semicircle's intersection with the real axis [26,37]. The analyzed RESR value is the lowest for cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite. The lowest value Rct and Rs for cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite is well in harmony with the results of CV and GCD analysis. The slope of the straight line for the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite is larger than others in the low-frequency area of the Nyquist plot, indicating improved capacitive behavior and reduced diffusion ion resistance. All these electrochemical performance reveals that cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite prepared from carcinogenic, harmful, and toxic waste material is an effective material for supercapacitor application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical evaluations of asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the electrochemical evaluation of each electrode material in a three-electrode configuration, clearly, cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite exhibits superior electrochemical to capacitive behavior, discharge time, and charge transfer phenomenon. To investigate more about the real-time use of this electrode composite, an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device was made employing CB as anode material and cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite as the cathode material. The particular assembly processes of the ASC device have been discussed in S3 (supporting information). The digital images of the cathode and anode used for the ASC as well as a schematic illustration of the ASC device are shown in Figs. S16 and S17, respectively.

All electrochemical analyses (CV, GCD, and EIS) of the ASC device were carried out in a 1 M TEABF4/ACN electrolyte. The CV analysis in 1 M TEABF4/ACN was carried out at various potential windows (up to 1.8 V) at 50 mV/s to assess the working potential of the ASC device, as portrayed in Fig. 7(a). All the CV curves have a similar nature during changing the potential windows from 0 to 1.8 V (Fig. 7(a)). Based on the CV study, the maximum operational potential of the device was found to be 1.8 V. CV plots of the device at different scan rates (10–200 mV/s) within 0–1.8 V potential limit are presented in Fig. 7(b). It has been noticed that the observed CV profiles greatly deviate from their ideal rectangular shape, proving the electrode material's largest contribution to pseudocapacitance. However, the device also produced a decent current at a slow scan rate (10 mV/s). The capacitive nature of this cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite as a cathode material has a significant potential for practical application, as shown by the CV study of the ASC device in 1 M TEABF4/ACN. The GCD profiles of the device within different potential windows confirm the symmetric triangular nature indicating the presence of maximum pseudocapacitive contribution (Fig. 7(c)). However, as the potential window was increased, the nature of GCD curves did not significantly change, supporting the earlier finding in the CV study.

To calculate the C
sc , an important parameter for the supercapacitor device, the GCD test was accomplished within the maximum operating potential (1.8 V) at multiple current densities (Fig. 7(d)). The specific capacitance of the ASC was calculated from the discharge time obtained from the GCD plot using eq. S7 (supporting information) [32,33]. Within the potential window of 1.8 V, a maximum C
sc of 161 F/g and an ED of 72 W h/kg were achieved. Comparing the ASC device to the three-electrode system, the working potential has improved. Even at 3 A/g current density, the device demonstrated the C
sc of 106 F/g indicating good rate capability of the device. The IR drop is observed to be lower in the case of the ASC device as compared to the three-electrode study, which allows the capacitive characteristics of the electrode to fully contribute to the operating potential. The EIS analysis shows the decrease of Rct value (0.85 Ω) as compared to the result in three-electrode configuration and the fitted Nyquist plot of the device is represented in Fig. 7(e). Fig. 7(f) displays a comparative Ragone plot to evaluate the effectiveness of the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite-based ASC device in comparison to previously reported similar studies. At an equivalent PD of 1798 W/kg, the cigarette particle-CB in-situ//CB device showed a maximum ED of 72 W h/kg. Also, the device demonstrated a high ED of 47 W h/kg even at a high PD of 5366 W/kg. The achieved ED is found to be more or analogous with the previously studied ASC devices like Carbon fiber derived from Cigarette Butt (CCB)/Vanadium Nitride (VN)//Activated Carbon (AC) [38], Tobacco waste-derived AC (TAC)//Tobacco waste derived AC (TAC) [39], MnO2@Porous carbon (PC)// MnO2@PC [40], and AC//Heteroatoms doped carbon sphere (SLCS) [41].

Additionally, the stability of the ASC device was evaluated by performing 10,000 consecutive GCD cycles within the potential window of 0–1.8 V. The ASC retained ~69 % capacitance after 10,000 GCD cycles (Fig. S18). The device's initial enhancement in capacitance retention was caused by the electrolyte's ions gradually dispersing into the electrode's pores, activating the anode and cathode materials. However, the lateral decrease in retention may be interpreted by the structural distortion of the electrode materials and the partial separation of the electrode material and current collector because of the continuous disinsertion and insertion of the electrolyte ions.

To evaluate the individual EDLC and pseudocapacitance contribution of the cigarette particle-CB in-situ//CB device, Trasatti's method was further implied and the corresponding plots are shown in Fig. S19. Fig. S20 demonstrates the bar plot of the ‘%’ of capacitance contributions, measured for the ASC device. The cigarette particle-CB in-situ//CB device exhibited more EDLC contribution as compared to the calculated EDLC contribution of cigarette particle-CB in-situ in a three-electrode configuration and this is mainly due to the use of EDLC-type CB as the negative electrode.

Additionally, a previously published approach [42] was used to calculate the volumetric ED of the ASC device. The superior volumetric ED measured by the ASC device was 0.56 mW h/cm3. In section S4 (supporting information), the volumetric ED's measuring procedures have been described in detail.

Based on the aforementioned discussion, for real-time applications, device testing was performed using a single device in 1 M TEABF4/ACN in two different current collectors (carbon fiber and carbon cloth). A single device (carbon fiber current collector) was charged for 30 s in a constant current with a max cut-off voltage set at 5.7 V. During discharging, the device was managed to run a 2-V mini fan (Fig. 8 (a, b)) up to 1 min 30 s. A single device also provided sufficient power to glow 43 red LEDs (IIT ISM) for approximately 3 min and corresponding figures are depicted in Fig. 8(c–f). In addition, the device fabricated by a carbon cloth current collector was also accomplished to power a 2-V fan and was able to illuminate 43 red LEDs (IIT ISM). Details on the practical uses for this ASC device are available in the supplementary information (Fig. S21).

In the current work, a carcinogenic and toxic pollutant, cigarette smoke has been successfully utilized for the fabrication of electrode material for electrochemical energy storage devices. The carbon-metal-based particulates obtained from cigarette smoke aerosol were combined distinctly with CB and CNT through in-situ and ex-situ processes. The CDs, obtained during the hydrothermal treatment of cigarette extract were characterized by PL analysis. It can be used for further application in nanoscience and nanotechnology. On the other hand, the electrochemical performances depict the superior ability of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite for supercapacitor electrodes with a C
sc of 248.75 F/g at 1 A/g in three electrode configurations. Moreover, the cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite-based asymmetric supercapacitor device delivered an exceptional electrochemical performance with an ED of 72 W h/kg. The facile synthetic route, adequate availability of this toxic waste material, and the numerous problem-solving abilities of this approach might be suitable for applications that are convenient in day-to-day life. Overall, this approach to developing efficient energy storage devices based on the hazardous pollutant aerosol of cigarette smoke provides a quick fix for two of the biggest issues of this century: environmental pollution and the energy crisis.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Supporting movie 1
Video Showing the synthesis of cigarette smoke aerosol.
Supporting movie 1
Supplementary material
Image 1

Digital images of different types of solutions for each cigarette extract (one to five cigarettes); Characterization techniques; UV–Vis and PL spectra of before and after hydrothermal distillates of different cigarette extracts; FESEM images of cigarette particle-CNT in-situ composite; EDX spectra of cigarette particle; XPS survey and XRD of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite; TEM and HRTEM images of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite; BET isotherm and pore size distribution of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite; CV plots of cigarette particle, cigarette particle-CB ex-situ, and cigarette particle-CNT in-situ composite in the three-electrode set-up; Trasatti method; ZETA potential measurements; DLS analysis; FESEM images of cigarette particle-CB in-situ composite after cyclic stability test; Equivalent circuit for fitted Nyquist plot; ASC device assembly process; Digital images of cathode and anode using different current collector; Illustration of ASC device; Cyclic stability plot of the ASC device; Trasatti analysis of the device; and Application of the device in details. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2024.114099.

Chandan Kumar Maity: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sourav Acharya: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Shrabani De: Visualization, Methodology, Data curation. Santosh Kumar Tiwari: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Conceptualization. Khusboo Kumari: Visualization, Formal analysis. Anupama Joy: Visualization, Formal analysis. Satyanarayan Sahoo: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Sumanta Sahoo: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis. Ganesh Chandra Nayak: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: For granting fellowship and working opportunities, C.K.M is grateful to Gachon University, Republic of Korea and S.A is grateful to IIT (ISM), Dhanbad, India.
